speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four. So what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding picture of my cousin, who was-- that was taken in 1935. I participated at the wedding. And the bottom though, in the middle, that's me. My mother with the hat, to the left and with the arrow. My two sisters to the right, standing. And my cousins next to me, who are my best friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Carei, the name of the city, which was not far from Satu Mare. This was taken from the wedding picture. This is the only picture I have from her. We enlarged it to have it clearer.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your mother? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szerena, she was a beautiful lady. My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was taken in the 1930s. I don't remember exactly when, what date. And this is the only picture I have from him. And I loved him very much. He was a great man. This is a picture of my oldest brother, the lower row, the bottom row in the middle. This picture I found here in the States, when he was in yeshiva in Europe, in Satu Mare. They wanted to have funds for the yeshiva. And they sent this picture to the States to, uh, raise some money. And by accident, I found this by some-- in somebody's house. They didn't even know that it was part of my family there. And I was very happy and excited to have this picture, because this is the only picture I have from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother did not survive. He died in the concentration camp, in Mauthausen. This picture is from my sister, my older sister. This was taken in 1936. They were engaged to get married. And my brother-in-law had a sister in the States that she sent-- he sent this picture and that's how I found it here. This sister was my-- the oldest from us, who was married and had two children. And he died in Auschwitz with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his-- her fiance's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was, uh, uh, no, David. The family name was David. And he died in, uh, by the border of, uh, Russia. He was taken to forced labor in Siberia. He died in Siberia. This picture was taken after the war in 1946. We were keeping company, seriously and I think I picked a handsome husband. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Frankfurt. This was taken in Frankfurt. This is my sister, who I survive together at concentration camp. She was older than I was, ten years older. She was very good to me. Thanks to her that I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1937. This picture was taken in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the gentleman with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedman, Eugene Friedman. He died in a car accident. And my sister passed away last year. This picture was taken in March 1947 and appeared in the This to, uh, prove that I received these packages while I was in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date was Tuesday, March 4, 1947. And the picture appeared in the paper and read, 'Former Postmaster General Jim Farley sends the 30,000th food package to displaced Jews abroad from General Post Office. Postmaster Albert Goldman cancels the stamps. Edith Niederman once got such packages and knows what they mean. She was in a displaced persons camp in Czechoslovakia.'

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken five years ago at my grandson's bar mitzvah. This is, uh, my children, my grandchildren, and my brother-in-law. I'm sitting next to the bar mitzvah boy. His name is David. And it was a beautiful simcha. This is a book, was printed in Israel in 1984 about our town, about Satu Mare-- the people who lived there, the occupation, what they had, and life in general in the city. I found the picture from my uncle and my cousin. This-- this is a picture of my uncle. Was taken in the 1930s. He's performing a wedding ceremony. He's saying the, uh, prayers. Also died in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the book, what they, uh, printed in Israel, 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is in the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: The book is, uh, about the history about our city and the Jewish people, what they did and, uh, what kind of occupation they had and in details what happened to them. This is also in the same book, printed. This is my first cousin who had seven children, was also taken to concentration camp. And none of them survived, not my cousin or her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the 1930s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the other woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: A maid. I want to introduce my husband, Moshe Hass, who I'm married to 47 years. And, uh, we have a happy marriage, I hope. [LAUGHS] And we come from the same background and we like the same things, more or less. We have a lot in common. And this is my son, Arnold, who is the second child. He's a wonderful boy, very good son. He gave us a lot of joy and happiness and adorable grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, Arnold-- [? Schlemie, ?] as I'm called in the home. I must say, thank God I've been endowed to be born in a family where I have two tremendous role models, as well as siblings, as well as uncles, and an aunt who recently passed away. We're very fortunate to have been raised in such a family. Um, I know it's a very short period of time that I have to speak, but I would like to say something in lieu of the fact that this is an interview regarding my parents who have been through the Holocaust. A child of the Holocaust, I would like to say that, um, there's-- I've never seen anything that, uh, would say embitterment or being bitter regarding what was done to them, which really amazes me, because I think all of us are sometimes-- uh, have a sense of, uh, anger at things that have been done to us. And my parents never uttered any anger towards what-- what was done to them. Only for one thing that really instilled in us is the fact that, um, a loss of people that would have enriched our lives, which we never met. Constantly speaking about their parents and their brothers and sisters and grandparents, and that part of life, which really is a loss that I feel I have, that-- that I wish I had. And now, looking at my children, seeing the richness that they gain by having uncles and aunts, I can relate to something that they've always told me that I-- I never could understand before because what we are is a product of our environment. And most importantly is the love that's endowed upon us by people that are close to us who love us. And that has a tremendous nurturing effect on us. And that is something that-- that I really begin to see what they told me. And I appreciate it. And, um, um, the two issues, again, I'll just repeat the fact that there's no sense of bitterment-- bitter, any proper word in English, I need help with, but no anger at what was done to them by the people that did it to them but rather focusing on the loss that was not gained by the people that would have been there. And again, that I'm very fortunate to have you both. And, and, and, uh, you're tremendous role models, your unselfishness, and your concern for your children. And the other thing that I would like to just point out, that--that my siblings as well, Shirley, my older sister and as well as other people who are children of Holocaust, uh, survivors, is that their total involvement and concern for the welfare-- welfare of the children was paramount. And that we, as children growing up, felt guilty to cause any aggravation to them in the slightest manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so a lot of things that I felt like rebelling when I was a child said, how can I do that after what they've been through? How could I do that? How could I do anything that would aggravate them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and talking to friends and family members, they all have the same feeling. They're like great kids in that regard. It's later on in life that we had a lot of anger that-- that was never brought forth. But that is a thing that really registers in my mind. And I looked at people who grew up in America, people of my parents' age, and when we spent summers, their concern was let's have a good time with the kids. Let's have-- just have a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: People who came from the Holocaust, focus was on how are the kids doing? Make sure they have education. Make sure you're doing your homework. Fun wasn't an issue. Fun wasn't part of life. Uh, fun never came into focus. And so as-- when I was a child, thinking of going to camp-- camp, that's fun. Who says we're supposed to have fun? We're supposed to have-- be good children. We're supposed to help out our parents. That was my sister's outlook on life. That was mine. That is everyone that I know who was a child of the Holocaust is, what can we do easier for the parents? Who cares what they're doing for us? We don't want anything from them. We just want to be concerned about our parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see the reverse with me raising my children. My concern is taking care of the children, let them have a good time. So I see a change in that regard. But I thank you both for doing a wonderful job. I hope you're not sad and you're proud of all your children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I am very happy to have been born in such a family with these parents. And I wish all you guys luck in your endeavor on-- on this monumental feat. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we-- I would like to introduce my family. This is my oldest, Shirley.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Goldie, my third.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second one was here before. And this is my granddaughter Aura.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say anything about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'd like to say what it was like to grow up as the oldest child of parents that had gone through the Holocaust. Um, the first word that would come to mind would be it was lonely. I would hear a lot about how they missed their parents, and if only their parents were here or their sister or their brother. There was the tremendous feeling of loneliness. And also, a feeling of responsibility that I had to be everything, like a friend, as well as a child, as well as the sister and brother, and the people that they missed, didn't leave much time to be a child. But there was also a tremendous appreciation for the basics-- a clean bed, peace, food, basic things that I think a lot of people took for granted but were very important to us at home. And I think that's different than other children that grow up in homes where they don't-- they're not haunted by the memories that my parents had and that they shared with us. Certainly, in the big picture, I'm very grateful for being brought up with a tremendous sense of morality, decency, kindness, especially in times that were not wonderful always to be, clean and straight, and decent, that that was more important than anything else. And with those kinds of guidelines, you could almost get through everything else. And, um, character issues were a lot more stressed and a lot more valued than financial issues. And, uh, very much I think for all four of us growing up in what we call the Hass household, we all share that kind of common sense of what's important. And it's something that binds us. And although we all have different lifestyles, those-- that type of thing runs through the thread of the family and is very much part of the threshold of the family. Particularly because my parents had seen really the down side of human nature and all the ugliness that comes through in times like that and yet, they survived. And part of the reason that they survived is because they never lost that sense of hope and sense of decency and sense of what was right, no matter what else was going on. That's really what I think is the most important, that I feel is special about the background I came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, I think growing-- as a child growing up, I always, uh, wondered-- tried to envision and many times used to have nightmares of if I would have had what it takes to, uh, survive what they went through. And yeah, I used to have nightmares as a child that, you know, the Germans would come and-- and what would happen to the family and how they would survive? And my responsibility as far as helping and taking care of my-- I had a little-- I have a little-- he was a little brother then, will-- will always be the little brother. But my responsibility, as far as taking care of him and what would happen if we were separated and how we would all survive, and if-- if I had what it takes to survive. Um, and always, um, to try to fill the void for our family, for our parents, for what they went through. And, uh, uh, it was hard to-- to be self-centered or stand for things that you really wanted, because you felt that you didn't want to give them extra pain or extra burdens. And you tried to make it as easy and the life as smooth as possible. And, um, I think there was a tremendous bond between all of us, and even, uh, whatever extended family we have in Israel-- my father has two surviving brothers-- and no matter what, somehow we always, um, have a bond to keep together. Um, and what always amazed me and what impresses me is how, in spite of everything, my parents and other Holocaust survivors that their lives were so fragmented and, um, they went through so much trauma, and yet, they were able to put the pieces together as best as they can and, uh, put together a home again, and, uh, focus on building. And that-- and that focusing on what was but going on. And you-- we look around at my friends-- most of my friends are survivors of Holocaust parents-- and they're still able to, um, try to grow from their experience and um, and, uh, and, and-- and rebuild. And, uh, in some ways, to learn what was, what happened, and, um, and be better for it, and-- and become stronger for it. And, um, just hope that it-- we-- it doesn't. It fades and it gets dim. And part of me doesn't want to allow it to, um, to fade and to hold onto it and not forget what-- what they went through and that we should learn from it and not allow it to happen again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have your grandparents and parents taught you anything about what your grandparents went through? AURA (GRANDDAUGHTER): Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that from all my children, Aura-- I have five children and Aura is my third and she is the most sensitive to, um, to what happened to her grandparents and asks most questions and-- and is always focusing on, you know, if she notices anything from the Holocaust, she's always focusing on, on, on past and trying to understand, um, how they could have survived it. And is very, very sensitive to it, I find, from-- from all of them. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the message that you've taught your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we are Jews, to be proud of it. That we are the best people. That we are the elite and that maybe that's why the world has such a hard time dealing with us. That whatever we touch turns to gold in terms of culture, education, intelligence, whatever it is. And that it's a badge to wear with honor and pride and never with shame. Because I think that, um, the European Jews, because of everything they had been through all those years, somehow were almost ashamed of who they were and were not able to fight back because of it. And I don't really know all the psychological reasons of why that happened but somehow, I think it did. And I think the Holocaust also somehow created for the people that went through it just a feeling of shame, because they were just so insulted all the time and called all those awful words. And, um, that's not the legacy that my children need to feel or experience. That it's something to be proud of and that we're special and better than everyone else. And, um, that's somehow, what for me has come out of everything that my parents went through. And in spite of everything, what they were able to build and to turn all this negativity into positive energy. Um, I think it's a wonderful legacy and a very rich thing to have. And, um, that's what I feel I want my children to get out of all of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that I'm proud. Um, I'm proud. I feel that, no matter what, they will-- we cannot be broken. That we're a nation-- we're a people, that they try and people resent or-- or have a problem with us. But I still feel that, uh, we've just become stronger and tougher and, uh, that we will survive no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
